sentences.text: Yeah.
No, it was like we were on some stop in between.
I mean, after Oederan when they-- we already-- and we were supposed to get off the train or something to relieve ourself or whatever.
And we were still behind, and a German-- he was not an SS.
It was already almost the end of the war, so they used very old people.
I don't know what you call them.
[GERMAN], I think.
And he had some bread or something, and he offered me some.
And Laura jumped out of the train, because on one hand she wanted me to have the bread.
On the other hand, she didn't want me to-- I mean, she wanted me to remain the proud self that I was.
I would never take.
And of course, I would never take anything then from a German.
I would rather die.
So I jumped off as well.
And it was-- I was feeling really good about it that I did not succumb to my hunger or whatever and took no bread.
That was one memory that I really vouch for that I remember.
That's how you are being shaped by circumstances.
Yeah, I guess so, but it didn't-- I mean, we didn't think anything good or bad about it.
We wanted it to be over, but we didn't know what the next moment will bring to us.
Because I mean, there was bombardment all the time.
We were close to Czechoslovakia, and some people probably thought they could still run away or something.
But we were not that strong anymore.
And this was quite horrible, because it was open box cars.
No food, no water, nothing, and people were really-- as I told you before, it got crazy by, I guess, by thirst or whatever.
And we were going around, and they couldn't put us into this-- I don't remember any-- which one.
I can find it out, which-- which concentration camp they wanted to put us to annihilate us.
So at a-- at a-- at a certain time, they took us out of the car and we started to walk.
And we walked and we walked.
And they put us back to Terezin, because we were close to the Czech border already in [INAUDIBLE] I think.
And we had to walk for I don't know how many hours.
And I remember we came back to Terezin.
And all of a sudden it was [WHOOSHING].
It was-- I mean, it was like [SIGH] talked of-- spoken or whatever.
Women from Terezin, women from Terezin.
Like, we are the women that came back like from-- and we were from Terezin.
Somebody recognized somebody hardly ever, because I tell you that the girls that I was living for three years in one place, they didn't recognize me.
We did not have any hair as yet.
We had a little hair, but it was a different way of looking.
And--
Yes.
Yes.
Terezin was-- for instance, yes.
A lot of the girls in the room that I used to be stayed behind, because we were one of the last transports to go out of Terezin.
It was only, as I told you, after this movie for the Red Cross and for propaganda purposes they-- I think there were only two transports, and that's it.
And then everything fell apart already.
But it was half a year, so half a year is quite a time to-- to go.
So-- so we came back to Terezin, but we were separated because we were sick and lice.
I don't know what [INAUDIBLE].
But then I remember that, again, whatever--
No, it must be close to the end of the war.
Yeah, yeah.
Definitely.
Definitely '45.
Yes.
But also one memory that all this standing around.
You know, we had to be what they called it cell appell, where they count you, and you stand for hours and hours.
And as I told you before, we didn't have much clothes.
[LAUGHS] And it wasn't-- it didn't get any better by time.
We were standing, and people used to, you know, like put something on their-- try to look healthy and wealthy and whatever.
So once we found something-- beet root I think it's called.
Yeah, and it has this reddish hue, so you put it on and you look like healthy and all this.
I mean, it-- it sounds crazy even today if you talk about it.
That was the way it was.
We were standing outside, and it was freezing, and you survived it.
And you went back and you had to work, and you didn't have any shoes or you did have shoes--
No, it was in the ammunition factory I told you about that we went to in Oederan.
When we were back in Terezin, we didn't work anymore.
It was already the end of the war.
That's why they couldn't do anything anymore.
So they just threw us into the entrance of Terezin, and then we marched into this group together.
And we started to spruce up to look better for the-- for our people, for our Terezin-- for our Terezin people.
So we tried to walk straight and-- and pretend that everything is OK.
And there I found also my aunt.
Not my aunt, my uncle.
My uncle, yes.
Steiner.
Leo Steiner, the brother of my mother who came at the time when we were already gone.
So he was very upset but he was happy to find me again.
I don't remember anymore who remembered whom.
But after that, Laura organized the children to go to some recreation home or whatever.
It was possibly to take, but it was-- it didn't go that fast.
But anyway she-- [SIGHS] she-- how do you say this?
I don't know.
Anyway, she found-- she put all the children together and we were sent to Czechoslovakia into places where the Germans were thrown out meanwhile.
It was--
This is after the war.
For me it was the moment I entered-- reentered Terezin, it was already coming to an end.
And also after all-- it was--
There was-- the Red Cross was there.
There was not anymore German.
It was already-- they couldn't annihilate us anymore.
They just-- that's why they didn't even put us in--
No, and I don't know who was there or stayed behind.
This I have no idea, because I stayed with Laura to the very end.
And she organized the children and everything, that-- that we're not sick or not sick that much [INAUDIBLE] so they could go out before anything else was organized.
Because they stayed behind in Terezin, and tried to find places and et cetera.
So it was very complicated time, and--
I don't remember this.
I only remember that we started-- yes, the Russians came, because I remember we had a few slogans already.
I don't know who told us these.
It was the [INAUDIBLE] army, and we worked on the Red Army.
[INAUDIBLE] was starting and all these things we had to-- we didn't have to.
I mean, we were happy to do it.

[LAUGHS] Believe me, nobody was anti-Russian then, [LAUGHS] because anything we could remember was that the Russian liberated us, so-- And Laura started to organize the children, and we-- and so-- and send us-- I don't know how-- anyway, to Czechoslovakia where we stayed in a recreational home and started to read and then recuperate and eat something and whatever.
OK.
Oh, no.
Where this I found here.
This is really funny, because a girlfriend, [INAUDIBLE], who stayed with me in Terezin, but she stayed behind in Terezin.
Well, it was then Eva Diamontova.
I don't even know exactly what her family-- you know, in Israel, you only know first names usually, so it's Eva.
And she's in [NON-ENGLISH].
And she has this little album where any kid wrote something in.
You know, it was the habit to do.
And one of those is here.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
OK.
So it says, [INAUDIBLE], [SPEAKING CZECH].
I'll read it line by line and try to translate.
So it's like Eva, life definitely is worth to live.
[SPEAKING CZECH].
It's only necessary to give it content, or do you say content?
Yeah.
To give it content.
[SPEAKING CZECH] If you should work with those possibilities that you have, you would definitely give it the best content.
[SPEAKING CZECH] Work according to your possibilities-- yes, possibilities-- and give away whatever you can.
[SPEAKING CZECH] Eva, remember sometimes at home-- underlined-- remember your cohabitant with whom you did a lot of-- have a lot of discussions, sometimes even quarreled.
But I hope that you also liked Greta Hofmeister well.
And that's it, and a few drawings accompany.
Oh, yes.
[INAUDIBLE] is another-- oh, this is even earlier.
This is from '42.
Also Terezin, the 13th of July '42.
It has a scale and has-- has a guitar.
See, my musical background.
And it says, [SPEAKING CZECH] Put your life always into the scale of C, because it has no coarseness.
[SPEAKING CZECH] Remember sometimes far away your cohabitant in Terezin-- in the Terezin Kinderheim, children's home.
[SPEAKING CZECH], which also was your girl friend, Greta Hofmeister.
Because in check you say if something is in C-- [CZECH] in major C, it has no crosses, so you don't bear any cross.
So you are--
Yeah.
So your life is easier.
You see?
Yeah.
I don't want to go into details.
This was terribly bureaucratic thing to find it.
Anyway, I found it.
Also, I found it because of Elena Makarova.
She did a beautiful exhibition of-- from Terezin-- from Bauhaus to Terezin, which was the teacher in Terezin.
She did-- she worked with children.
And this way, I found drawings of my sister after 40 years.
Which she had done in Terezin, of course.
And the state refused-- remained behind in Terezin, and it came to the Jewish Museum in Prague.
And by chance or not by chance or via Elena Makarova, I found the drawings and I got copies of it after long and bureaucratic way.
And I have--
This is like a-- the last greetings of my sister.
I don't know exactly when, but it was quite obvious after everything was more or less organized.
I don't know whether it took two months, three months, or a year.

But before I came back to-- before I came back to my parents, before I came to Palestine-- it was still Palestine because I came in '46 to Palestine-- I already knew that I have no sister and no relatives left.
But my parents were there in Palestine, and--
Well, of course, Laura organized the children, and then she organized the place for-- in Prague for me to come back to to study or whatever.
And I make it short, because it was a nice time and OK.
Anyway, it was-- it was a very Christian gentleman who-- his name is Psemisl Pitel-- who took care of all those children.
Not necessarily only Jewish children, because he was a humanist and he wanted every children to have a better time or whatever.
But it was also very funny, in a way, because he was very religious.
And he tried to explain to us what death meant.
Death for-- for us who went through a lot of different kind of death, we couldn't even-- I mean, I couldn't relate to it somehow.
It's-- I found it absurd, really absurd even then.
Because I remember, for instance, he told us that death is like you go from one room to the other and leave your coat behind, and that's it.

And I said to myself-- I was then still very quiet and I couldn't speak to the person who was so completely nothing our kind of life to tell him that death meant a lot of things, but definitely not leaving a coat behind.
So that was only one little example how difficult it was for somebody to comprehend who wasn't there or hasn't been near it.
Also, he was really a very, very good person.
He even brought milk to children to Terezin, and I don't know what different kind of dangers.
He was arrested when in Germany, and he was arrested from the Czech communist.
He was always arrest-- he was really a fantastic person.
So I don't want to say anything against him, but it was-- I just wanted to show you that it was impossible.
It was also one of the reasons that when I finally did come to Palestine, I couldn't talk to my parents.
Because what I, as only 15, 16-year-old could tell to my parents about what happened, and how could I tell them how their child died?
How their mother died or whatever?
It was impossible.
And I knew I couldn't talk.
This I knew.
I was grown up enough to know that it wasn't possible to understand anyway.
And also, why should I put even more anxiety and sadness to my parents whom I still didn't even know?
Because I really didn't know them, and they were completely different from what I thought they were going to be.
And the whole situation was quite complicated in Palestine in '46, where it was really-- it was a desert.
There was almost no language.
And you could not study-- all I wanted to do was, for instance, was to study.
So over-- I mean, who could do it for me?
I mean, how could I-- I couldn't even-- I didn't have any money and my parents didn't have no money.
There was also no schools really, so I started to study English.
And that was also good-- English and Hebrew, of course.
Could we go back a bit--
--because we we ended up in Palestine before we got there.
After the war.
Yeah, OK.
So then the man stayed there still, because there were a lot of children who didn't have any solution for their life.
This was in Czech.
It was Czech-- how do you call this-- [CZECH].
Not country places.
It was like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know the name now.
It was not a country place.
It was for-- very rich people have these very fancy places with a lot of gardens and stuff, and they were open [INAUDIBLE] for children that survived.
Anyway--
It was Czech-- I guess it was Czech organization, but Czech and Jewish.
And because it was a co-production of-- of this-- the places.
They were very beautiful, a lot of wooden-- a lot of forests in between, so good fresh air and whatever.
And a lot of books I found there, which immediately I stole, of course, and then brought back to Laura because we wanted to read.
It was one of the things we did also.
We stole books, because we were so hungry to have something to read.
It was [INAUDIBLE].
And fortunately, we knew German, so who knew German in Czechoslovakia?
So-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] Sorry, do you remember any specific books?
Oh, yes.
Jakob Wassermann.
I know-- don't know the name of the-- title of the book, but I know it was-- it was something [INAUDIBLE].
It was then very popular, or not popular but very well known.
But nobody reads Wassermann at the moment.
It was also Jewish also, but nobody knows about it anymore.
This is like-- you know, history changes so fast, and especially now who's reading still.
Anyway, I went back to Czech-- Laura created a head of-- found a flat in the place where her sister also came, and I had a room.
So she, of course, invited-- I mean, I stayed with her.
And--
In Prague.
In Prague.
Yeah.
The capitol of Czechoslovakia.
And I very much wanted to go to school, to a normal school with all the children, with all the Czech children.
And Laura, being clever but very-- I was very upset about it-- she said no.
You have to go to an English school, and to-- because there was no possibility to study Hebrew then or there was no Hebrew almost.
But she said, you-- you are going to Palestine, so you have to study English.
Because Czech is no good for us.
I want to be like anybody else.
I don't want to be an outsider again.
But she was a very clever.
She put me-- and she made me study music.
I mean, I studied piano playing with [INAUDIBLE], another survivor, and I went to an English language school.
So here is my English.
This is the good-- the foundation of my English was already in Prague where I stayed a year.
Yeah, it was very hard after the war.
There was not immediate contact, but I knew they were alive.
That I knew.
They did not know.
They did not know who survived and if survived then they still saw them.
But my uncle wrote to them, and there-- he was more grown up.
I was so-- I wrote to them, of course, in German, because there was, again, this problem of language, which I spoke only Czech and I lived in Czechoslovakia.
But my parents living in Palestine spoke German.
So here we go again.
But as I told you, Laura was, and she-- and I thank God and her-- that she really said, this you are going to do if you like it or not, because there is no sense in you going to a Czech school now.
You-- you don't go to any party or even the youth group of [INAUDIBLE].
You are going to study music and English.
And later on, we are going to find out if it will not be only-- she even-- it was very hard for me, because she sent me away.
Actually, she sent me to Palestine.
I never-- I mean, I didn't think they are guilty of anything, never.
Never in my life.
But I didn't feel I have to go to Palestine.
But I wanted to be like anybody else.
Well, lucky I was not, and lucky she sent me against my will to Palestine.
No, she had a sister, and she had a life.
And she had nothing, and so she had to build her life before it.
There was also nothing to go to.
It was-- I mean, you have to remember it was April '46.
There was not even Israel as yet.
That's another story.
So that's it.
Almost a year.
Yes, I studied in Prague.
No, it was just the English Language School.

It was a language school I know I remember all these nursery rhymes and all these different-- but it was a very good foundation, I think, because it also made me curious enough to start reading in English.
So the first book I bought in Jerusalem was but finally in English.
Not that I understood it, but I took dictionaries, and then I understood it.
And it was really-- ah.
I was-- I still love Van Gogh.
[LAUGH] Yeah.
So it was not an easy time.
If you are going to Palestine already, maybe go and stay in Palestine for the time being.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, it was the Red Cross train.
We were a whole train of children and survivors who went via-- via Paris.
This was also a story-- via Paris to Haifa and-- I mean, to Palestine.
We got--
This was legal, completely legal.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE] repatriate whatever train.
We went onto Marseilles, and from Marseilles to Paris-- or it'd be different now from-- I don't know which way you go first.
Anyway, from Marseilles you shipped [INAUDIBLE] to Haifa. yes.
There was of course, no plane over there.
Yeah.
It took quite some time, and--
And she put you-- saw you onto the train?
She put me onto the train, yes, and I was very cross with her.
Until I understood what-- how-- how terrible it was for her, because for her it was everything.
I mean, she lost whatever she had but her older sister.
And I was really her-- actually her child.
And she was great enough to-- to send me away.
And she told me also that the moment it would not be only an emotional thing, but a thing that you really thought through to the end-- you can always come back.
Of course, I never wanted to go back when it was already at that time.
Yeah.
But unfortunately, the communists didn't let me in, so I could not meet there any more.
But we wrote to each other, and she knew about me.
And she wrote to my-- she also introduced me to my parents.
I mean, she wrote to my parents what are my-- my possibilities, what I'm good at, and what I could do and what a person I am.
And if I would [INAUDIBLE] I would not-- I would show you the letter.
But I can't find it at the moment.
The trip-- that I tried to completely forget it.
What is the end of the trip that I wanted to enjoy the trip.
We were finally ourselves.
It was like-- it was Juda Bakwan, Alex Schachter, [INAUDIBLE] Furthova, and me.
We were like four people who were coming to Palestine.
Parents or-- no, no.
Not parents.
Juda had only-- had a sister in [NON-ENGLISH].
Maybe it wasn't [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
Maybe in [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
Anyway, she-- she found her sister here.
Alex found his sister here.
And Eva-- I don't know who she found-- if she found.
She probably did, otherwise she wouldn't come to Palestine then.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, you interrupted me and I don't forget where I was.
It's OK, but I don't forget what we talked about.
Oh, yes.
We wanted completely-- this was-- I was great at that.
I could always like forget at the moment anything wrong and enjoy that moment.
And that was very important, because we came to Paris.
We got some money for food.
Of course, we didn't buy food.
We thought-- for the first time, we bought books.
And we went to the-- I know we had a little hotel or something where we stayed until they-- we could go on.
We were so happy.
We never-- it was so unimportant that we had nothing to eat.
We had a few sardines for the way and something.
There was nothing to have.
It was really complete crazy time, even in Paris.
But I still-- a few years ago, I still had the books.
They were whole, terrible copies.
You know, those productions where unbelievably bad.
But it was such a joy to have your own book and to open it and to enjoy something completely normal.
It was unbelievable.
And the freedom of it.
You went-- we were so fearless when-- we didn't know we are supposed to be afraid of something.
You were not.
You were happy to be alive.
Not that it was easy afterwards to-- to recognize your parents and-- and find another kind of way.
And actually, we had a very, very nice relation after a few time-- after I left my parents to study music in Jerusalem, and until the war came here in Palestine.
And then Israel, I also was wounded here.
But that was very easy and happy time nevertheless.
We were very, very good nation then.
We were all together.
And also, the way to find my parents was very difficult and compliment--

--complicated in the beginning, but it really was a-- it sounds-- I mean, it sounds like a cliche, but really love really bridged-- [INAUDIBLE] it bridged so beautifully that we found a very, very good relationship in the end, and that's it.
Oh, so you're finished.
That's it.
No.
